facade_colour: gray ashlar-pattern stone veneer facade with cream entry surround and vivid red metal-clad mansard dormers

hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Row, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row of identical residences, the properties retain original building features, including their shared roofline, gables, fenestration pattern with segmentally arched window openings, and ground floor bay windows. 106 and 110 Oxford Street retain their original dichromatic brick cladding while 108 Oxford Street has been overclad. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way | Residential | Fine-grain Victorian rowhouses | Quiet

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: gray ashlar-pattern stone veneer facade with cream entry surround and vivid red metal-clad mansard dormers
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Heavily altered facade but well maintained, with clean stone veneer, modern hardscaping, and crisp replacement windows.
photo_observations.window_details: Main facade has two large rectangular upper windows, one arched first-floor window with stone arch, and two narrow dormer windows in the red roof.
photo_observations.door_details: Single dark red entry door recessed within an ornate cream-painted surround and small stoop.
photo_observations.cornice_details: Minimal trim; visual emphasis is on the mansard roof and molded door surround.
photo_observations.porch_details: Short raised stoop with black railings and paved front walk.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney visible from the front view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Molded classical-style entry surround, arched window trim, and two small pointed mansard dormers create the facade character.
photo_observations.roof_type_observed: Mansard-like roof with two front dormers
photo_observations.signage_observed: House number 119 appears on the bin and entry surround despite the batch address using 110 Oxford St.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Substantial recladding with stone veneer, replacement windows, and a rebuilt mansard roof significantly alter the original heritage expression.
photo_observations.notes: The photographed facade is marked 119 Oxford St in IMG_20260315_151121824_HDR.jpg; I treated it as the batch's 110 Oxford record but noted the numbering discrepancy.

facade_description: 110 Oxford St presents a bay-and-gable domestic facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, row, bay-and-gable typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed in a gray ashlar-pattern stone veneer facade with cream entry surround and vivid red metal-clad mansard dormers palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include original windows, decorative brick, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a row of identical residences, the properties retain original building features, including their shared roofline, gables, fenestration pattern with segmentally arched window openings, and ground floor bay windows. 106 and 110 Oxford Street retain their original dichromatic brick cladding while 108 Oxford Street has been overclad. Contextually, each property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. Each property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.
